subject: Physics
question_text: Consider Experiment 6.2. What would you do to obtain a large deflection of the galvanometer? How would you demonstrate the presence of an induced current in the absence of a galvanometer?
question_html:
Consider Experiment 6.2.

  1. What would you do to obtain a large deflection of the galvanometer?
  2. How would you demonstrate the presence of an induced current in the absence of a galvanometer?
solution_text: To obtain a large deflection, one or more of the following steps can be taken: Use a rod made of soft iron inside the coil C 2 , Connect the coil to a powerful battery, and Move the arrangement rapidly towards the test coil C 1 . Replace the galvanometer by a small bulb, the kind one finds in small torchlight. The relative motion between the two coils will cause the bulb to glow and thus demonstrate the presence of induced current. In experimental physics, one must learn to innovate. Michael Faraday who is ranked as one of the best experimentalists ever was legendary for his innovative skills.
solution_html:
1. To obtain a large deflection, one or more of the following steps can be taken:
    1. Use a rod made of soft iron inside the coil C2,
    2. Connect the coil to a powerful battery, and
    3. Move the arrangement rapidly towards the test coil C1.
  2. Replace the galvanometer by a small bulb, the kind one finds in small torchlight. The relative motion between the two coils will cause the bulb to glow and thus demonstrate the presence of induced current.
    In experimental physics, one must learn to innovate. Michael Faraday who is ranked as one of the best experimentalists ever was legendary for his innovative skills.

subject: Physics
question_text: A square loop of side 10 cm and resistance 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is placed vertically in the east-west plane. A uniform magnetic field of 0.10 T is set up across the plane in the north-east direction. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in 0.70 s at a steady rate. Determine the magnitudes of induced emf and current during this time-interval.
question_html: A square loop of side 10 cm and resistance 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is placed vertically in the east-west plane. A uniform magnetic field of 0.10 T is set up across the plane in the north-east direction. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in 0.70 s at a steady rate. Determine the magnitudes of induced emf and current during this time-interval.
solution_text: The angle {tex}\theta{/tex} made by the area vector of the coil with the magnetic field is 45°. From Eq. the initial magnetic flux is {tex}\Phi=B A \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.1 \times 10^{-2}}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{Wb}{/tex} Final flux {tex}\phi_{min}=0{/tex} The change in flux is brought about in 0.70 s. From eq. ( {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \Phi_{B}}{\mathrm{~d} t}{/tex} ) the magnitude of the induced emf during time interval is given by {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{\left|\Delta \phi_{B}\right|}{\Delta t}=\frac{|\phi-0|}{\Delta t}=\frac{10^{-3}}{\sqrt{2} \times 0.7}=1.0 \mathrm{mV}{/tex} And the magnitude of the current during the time interval is given by: {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{10^{-3} \mathrm{V}}{0.5 \Omega}=2 \mathrm{mA}{/tex} Note that the earth’s magnetic field also produces a flux through the loop. But it is a steady field (which does not change within the time span of the experiment) and hence does not induce any emf.
solution_html:
The angle {tex}\theta{/tex} made by the area vector of the coil with the magnetic field is 45°. From Eq. the initial magnetic flux is
{tex}\Phi=B A \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{0.1 \times 10^{-2}}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{Wb}{/tex}
Final flux {tex}\phi_{min}=0{/tex}
The change in flux is brought about in 0.70 s. From eq. ({tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \Phi_{B}}{\mathrm{~d} t}{/tex}) the magnitude of the induced emf  during time interval is given by
{tex}\varepsilon=\frac{\left|\Delta \phi_{B}\right|}{\Delta t}=\frac{|\phi-0|}{\Delta t}=\frac{10^{-3}}{\sqrt{2} \times 0.7}=1.0 \mathrm{mV}{/tex}
And the magnitude of the current during the time interval is given by:
{tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{10^{-3} \mathrm{V}}{0.5 \Omega}=2 \mathrm{mA}{/tex}
Note that the earth’s magnetic field also produces a flux through the loop. But it is a steady field (which does not change within the time span of the experiment) and hence does not induce any emf.

subject: Physics
question_text: A circular coil of radius 10 cm, 500 turns and resistance 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is placed with its plane perpendicular to the horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field. It is rotated about its vertical diameter through 180° in 0.25 s. Estimate the magnitudes of the emf and current induced in the coil. The horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field at the place is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T
question_html: A circular coil of radius 10 cm, 500 turns and resistance 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is placed with its plane perpendicular to the horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field. It is rotated about its vertical diameter through 180° in 0.25 s. Estimate the magnitudes of the emf and current induced in the coil. The horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field at the place is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 T
solution_text: Initial flux through the coil, {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B} \text { initial }}=B A \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times\left(\pi \times 10^{-2}\right) \times \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=3 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Wb}{/tex} Final flux after the rotation, {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B} \text { (final })}=3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times\left(\pi \times 10^{-2}\right) \times \cos 180^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=-3 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Wb}{/tex} Therefore, the estimated value of the induced emf is, {tex}\epsilon=N \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{500 \times\left[6 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right] }{ 0.25}{/tex} {tex}=3.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{V}{/tex} {tex}I= \frac {\varepsilon}{ R}=1.9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{A}{/tex}
solution_html:
Initial flux through the coil,
{tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B} \text { initial }}=B A \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}=3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times\left(\pi \times 10^{-2}\right) \times \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=3 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Wb}{/tex}
Final flux after the rotation,
{tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B} \text { (final })}=3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times\left(\pi \times 10^{-2}\right) \times \cos 180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=-3 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Wb}{/tex}
Therefore, the estimated value of the induced emf is,
{tex}\epsilon=N \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{500 \times\left[6 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right] }{ 0.25}{/tex}
{tex}=3.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{V}{/tex}
{tex}I= \frac {\varepsilon}{ R}=1.9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: In Figure, shows planar loops of different shapes moving out of or into a region of a magnetic field which is directed normal to the plane of the loop away from the reader. Determine the direction of induced current in each loop using Lenz’s law.
question_html: In Figure, shows planar loops of different shapes moving out of or into a region of a magnetic field which is directed normal to the plane of the loop away from the reader. Determine the direction of induced current in each loop using Lenz’s law.
solution_text: The magnetic flux through the rectangular loop abcd increases, due to the motion of the loop into the region of a magnetic field According to Lenz's law, this increase in flux is opposed by the induced current. The induced current must flow along the path bcdab so that it opposes the increasing flux. Due to the outward motion, magnetic flux through the triangular loop abc decreases due to which the induced current flows along bacd, so as to oppose the change in flux. As the magnetic flux decreases due to motion of the irregularly shaped loop abcd out of the region of the magnetic field, the induced current flows along cdabc, so as to oppose change in flux. Note that there is no induced current as long as the loops are completely inside or outside the region of the magnetic field.
solution_html:
1. The magnetic flux through the rectangular loop abcd increases, due to the motion of the loop into the region of a magnetic field According to Lenz's law, this increase in flux is opposed by the induced current.   The induced current must flow along the path bcdab so that it opposes the increasing flux.
  2. Due to the outward motion, magnetic flux through the triangular loop abc decreases due to which the induced current flows along bacd, so as to oppose the change in flux.
  3. As the magnetic flux decreases due to motion of the irregularly shaped loop abcd out of the region of the magnetic field, the induced current flows along cdabc, so as to oppose change in flux. Note that there is no induced current as long as the loops are completely inside or outside the region of the magnetic field.

subject: Physics
question_text: A closed-loop is held stationary in the magnetic field between the north and south poles of two permanent magnets held fixed. Can we hope to generate current in the loop by using very strong magnets? A closed-loop move normal to the constant electric field between the plates of a large capacitor. Is a current induced in the loop? when it is wholly inside the region between the capacitor plates. when it is partially outside the plates of the capacitor? The electric field is normal to the plane of the loop. A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field region (Fig.) to a field-free region with a constant velocity v . In which loop do you expect the induced emf to be constant during the passage out of the field region? The field is normal to the loops. Predict the polarity of the capacitor in the situation described by Figure.
question_html:
1. A closed-loop is held stationary in the magnetic field between the north and south poles of two permanent magnets held fixed. Can we hope to generate current in the loop by using very strong magnets?
  2. A closed-loop move normal to the constant electric field between the plates of a large capacitor. Is a current induced in the loop?
    1. when it is wholly inside the region between the capacitor plates.
    2. when it is partially outside the plates of the capacitor? The electric field is normal to the plane of the loop.
  3. A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field region (Fig.) to a field-free region with a constant velocity v. In which loop do you expect the induced emf to be constant during the passage out of the field region? The field is normal to the loops.
  4. Predict the polarity of the capacitor in the situation described by Figure.
solution_text: No. However strong the magnet may be, current can be induced only by changing the magnetic flux through the loop. Or, The electricity get generated due to the phenomenon of electromagnetic induction. This happens due to changing of magnetic force by changing the position of the magnet but in this case, the magnet is held stationary. So no current is generated. No current is induced in either case. Current can not be induced by changing the electric flux. The induced emf is expected to be constant only in the case of the rectangular loop. In the case of a circular loop, the rate of change of the area of the loop during its passage out of the field region is not constant, hence induced emf will vary accordingly. The polarity of plate ‘A’ will be positive with respect to plate ‘B’ in the capacitor.
solution_html:
1. No. However strong the magnet may be, current can be induced only by changing the magnetic flux through the loop. Or, The electricity get generated due to the phenomenon of electromagnetic induction. This happens due to changing of magnetic force by changing the position of the magnet but in this case, the magnet is held stationary. So no current is generated.
  2. No current is induced in either case. Current can not be induced by changing the electric flux.
  3. The induced emf is expected to be constant only in the case of the rectangular loop. In the case of a circular loop, the rate of change of the area of the loop during its passage out of the field region is not constant, hence induced emf will vary accordingly.
  4. The polarity of plate ‘A’ will be positive with respect to plate ‘B’ in the capacitor.

subject: Physics
question_text: A metallic rod of 1 m length is rotated with a frequency of 50 rev/s, with one end hinged at the centre and the other end at the circumference of a circular metallic ring of radius 1 m, about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring (Fig.). A constant and uniform magnetic field of 1 T parallel to the axis is present everywhere. What is the emf between the centre and the metallic ring?
question_html: A metallic rod of 1 m length is rotated with a frequency of 50 rev/s, with one end hinged at the centre and the other end at the circumference of a circular metallic ring of radius 1 m, about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring (Fig.). A constant and uniform magnetic field of 1 T parallel to the axis is present everywhere. What is the emf between the centre and the metallic ring?
solution_text: Method I As the rod is rotated, free electrons in the rod move towards the outer end due to Lorentz force and get distributed over the ring. Thus, the resulting separation of charges produces an emf across the ends of the rod. At a certain value of emf, there is no more flow of electrons and a steady-state is reached. and v is the linear velocity of element. Using Eq. {tex}-B l \frac{d x}{d t}=B l v{/tex} , the magnitude of the emf generated across a length dr of the rod as it moves at right angles to the magnetic field is given by {tex}\mathrm{d} \varepsilon{/tex} = Bvr. Hence, {tex}\varepsilon=\int \mathrm{d} \varepsilon=\int_{0}^{R} B v \mathrm{d} r=\int_{0}^{R} B \omega r \mathrm{d} r=\frac{B \omega R^{2}}{2}{/tex} Note that we have used {tex}v=\omega r{/tex} . This gives {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{1}{2} \times 1.0 \times 2 \pi \times 50 \times\left(1^{2}\right){/tex} = 157 V Method II To calculate the emf, we can imagine a closed-loop OPQ in which points O and P are connected with a resistor R, and OQ is the rotating rod. The potential difference across the resistor is then equal to the induced emf and equals B {tex}\times{/tex} (rate of change of the area of the loop). If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the rod and the radius of the circle at P at time t, the area of the sector OPQ is given by {tex}\pi R^{2} \times \frac{\theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2} R^{2} \theta{/tex} where R is the radius of the circle. Hence, the induced emf is {tex}\varepsilon=B \times \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{1}{2} R^{2} \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} B R^{2} \frac{d \theta}{d t}=\frac{B \omega R^{2}}{2}{/tex} [Note: {tex}\frac{d \theta}{d t}=\omega=2 \pi v{/tex} ] This expression is identical to the expression obtained by Method I and we get the same value of {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} , as we get from the first method = 157 V
solution_html:
Method I
As the rod is rotated, free electrons in the rod move towards the outer end due to Lorentz force and get distributed over the ring. Thus, the resulting separation of charges produces an emf across the ends of the rod. At a certain value of emf, there is no more flow of electrons and a steady-state is reached. and v is the linear velocity of element. Using Eq. {tex}-B l \frac{d x}{d t}=B l v{/tex}, the magnitude of the emf generated across a length dr of the rod as it moves at right angles to the magnetic field is given by {tex}\mathrm{d} \varepsilon{/tex} = Bvr.
Hence,
{tex}\varepsilon=\int \mathrm{d} \varepsilon=\int_{0}^{R} B v \mathrm{d} r=\int_{0}^{R} B \omega r \mathrm{d} r=\frac{B \omega R^{2}}{2}{/tex}
Note that we have used {tex}v=\omega r{/tex}. This gives
{tex}\varepsilon=\frac{1}{2} \times 1.0 \times 2 \pi \times 50 \times\left(1^{2}\right){/tex}
= 157 V
Method II
To calculate the emf, we can imagine a closed-loop OPQ in which points O and P are connected with a resistor R, and OQ is the rotating rod. The potential difference across the resistor is then equal to the induced emf and equals B {tex}\times{/tex} (rate of change of the area of the loop). If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the rod and the radius of the circle at P at time t, the area of the sector OPQ is given by
{tex}\pi R^{2} \times \frac{\theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2} R^{2} \theta{/tex}
where R is the radius of the circle. Hence, the induced emf is
{tex}\varepsilon=B \times \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{1}{2} R^{2} \theta\right]{/tex}{tex}=\frac{1}{2} B R^{2} \frac{d \theta}{d t}=\frac{B \omega R^{2}}{2}{/tex}
[Note: {tex}\frac{d \theta}{d t}=\omega=2 \pi v{/tex}]
This expression is identical to the expression obtained by Method I and we get the same value of {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}, as we get from the first method = 157 V

subject: Physics
question_text: A wheel with 10 metallic spokes each 0.5 m long is rotated with a speed of 120 rev/min in a plane normal to the horizontal component of earth’s magnetic field. If the magnitude of the field is 0.4 G at the place, what is the induced emf between the axle and the rim of the wheel? Note that 1 G = 10 -4 T
question_html: A wheel with 10 metallic spokes each 0.5 m long is rotated with a speed of 120 rev/min in a plane normal to the horizontal component of earth’s magnetic field. If the magnitude of the field is 0.4 G at the place, what is the induced emf between the axle and the rim of the wheel? Note that 1 G = 10-4T
solution_text: The area covered by an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}A = \pi {r^2}\frac{\theta }{{2\pi }} = \frac{{{r^2}}}{2}\theta{/tex} The induced EMF, {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = \frac{{d(BA)}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = B\frac{{{r^2}}}{2}\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} = B\frac{{{r^2}}}{2}\omega{/tex} Here, {tex}\nu{/tex} = 120 rev/min = 2 rev/s {tex}\omega = 2\pi \nu = 4\pi \,rad/s{/tex} r = 0.5 m B = 0.4 G {tex} = 0.4 \times {10^{ - 4}}T{/tex} {tex}E = \frac{1}{2}B{r^2}\omega{/tex} {tex}E = \frac{{2\pi \times 2 \times 0.4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times {{(0.5)}^2}}}{2}{/tex} {tex} = 6.28 \times {10^{ - 5}}V{/tex} As all spokes are connected in parallel between the axle and the rim, therefore net e.m.f. induced is the same as that induced across the ends of each spoke.. Hence, the EMF will be {tex}6.28 \times {10^{ - 5}}V{/tex} .
solution_html:
The area covered by an angle {tex}\theta{/tex},
{tex}A = \pi {r^2}\frac{\theta }{{2\pi }} = \frac{{{r^2}}}{2}\theta{/tex}
The induced EMF, {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = \frac{{d(BA)}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = B\frac{{{r^2}}}{2}\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} = B\frac{{{r^2}}}{2}\omega{/tex}
Here, {tex}\nu{/tex} = 120 rev/min = 2 rev/s
{tex}\omega = 2\pi \nu = 4\pi \,rad/s{/tex}
r = 0.5 m
B = 0.4 G {tex} = 0.4 \times {10^{ - 4}}T{/tex}
{tex}E = \frac{1}{2}B{r^2}\omega{/tex}
{tex}E = \frac{{2\pi \times 2 \times 0.4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times {{(0.5)}^2}}}{2}{/tex}
{tex} = 6.28 \times {10^{ - 5}}V{/tex}
As all spokes are connected in parallel between the axle and the rim, therefore net e.m.f. induced is the same as that induced across the ends of each spoke.. Hence, the EMF will be {tex}6.28 \times {10^{ - 5}}V{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_text: Two concentric circular coils, one of small radius r 1 and the other of large radius r 2 , such that r 1 << r 2 , are placed co-axially with centres coinciding. Obtain the mutual inductance of the arrangement.
question_html: Two concentric circular coils, one of small radius r1 and the other of large radius r2, such that r1 << r2, are placed co-axially with centres coinciding. Obtain the mutual inductance of the arrangement.
solution_text: Let a current I 2 flow through the outer circular coil. The field at the centre of the coil is B 2 = {tex}\frac {\mu I_2}{2r_2}{/tex} . Since the other co-axially placed coil has a very small radius, B 2 may be considered constant over its cross-sectional area. Hence {tex}\phi_{1}=\pi r_{1}^{2} B_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}} I_{2}{/tex} Thus, {tex}M_{12}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex} From eq. M 12 = M 21 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let a current I2 flow through the outer circular coil. The field at the centre of the coil is B2 = {tex}\frac {\mu I_2}{2r_2}{/tex}. Since the other co-axially placed coil has a very small radius, B2 may be considered constant over its cross-sectional area. Hence
{tex}\phi_{1}=\pi r_{1}^{2} B_{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}} I_{2}{/tex}
Thus,
{tex}M_{12}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex}
From eq.
M12 = M21 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Obtain the expression for the magnetic energy stored in a solenoid in terms of magnetic field B, area A and length l of the solenoid. How does this magnetic energy compare with the electrostatic energy stored in a capacitor?
question_html:
1. Obtain the expression for the magnetic energy stored in a solenoid in terms of magnetic field B, area A and length l of the solenoid.
  2. How does this magnetic energy compare with the electrostatic energy stored in a capacitor?
solution_text: the total amount of work done in the form of the magnetic energy is {tex}U_{B}=\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} L\left(\frac{B}{\mu_{o} n}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}\right)^{2}{/tex} , (Since B = {tex}\mu_{o} n I{/tex} , for a solenoid) {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}\right)^{2}{/tex} , [from eq L. {tex}=\mu_{0} n^{2} A l{/tex} ] {tex}=\frac{1}{2 \mu_{0}} B^{2} A l{/tex} The magnetic energy per unit volume is, {tex}u_{B}=\frac{U_{B}}{V}{/tex} (where V is volume that contains flux) V=Al {tex}=\frac{U_{B}}{A l}{/tex} We have already obtained the relation for the electrostatic energy stored per unit volume in a parallel plate capacitor. {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} In both the cases energy is proportional to the square of the field strength. Equations {tex}=\frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex} and {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} have been derived for special cases: a solenoid and a parallel plate capacitor, respectively. But they are general and valid for any region of space in which a magnetic field or/and an electric field exist.
solution_html:
1. the total amount of work done in the form of the magnetic energy is
    {tex}U_{B}=\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} L\left(\frac{B}{\mu_{o} n}\right)^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}\right)^{2}{/tex}, (Since B = {tex}\mu_{o} n I{/tex}, for a solenoid)
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}\right)^{2}{/tex}, [from eq L. {tex}=\mu_{0} n^{2} A l{/tex}]
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2 \mu_{0}} B^{2} A l{/tex}
  2. The magnetic energy per unit volume is,
    {tex}u_{B}=\frac{U_{B}}{V}{/tex} (where V is volume that contains flux) V=Al
    {tex}=\frac{U_{B}}{A l}{/tex}

We have already obtained the relation for the electrostatic energy stored per unit volume in a parallel plate capacitor.
{tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex}
In both the cases energy is proportional to the square of the field strength. Equations {tex}=\frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex} and {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} have been derived for special cases: a solenoid and a parallel plate capacitor, respectively. But they are general and valid for any region of space in which a magnetic field or/and an electric field exist.

subject: Physics
question_text: Kamla peddles a stationary bicycle. The pedals of the bicycle are attached to a 100 turn coil of area 0.10 m 2 . The coil rotates at half a revolution per second and it is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.01 T perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the coil. What is the maximum voltage generated in the coil?
question_html: Kamla peddles a stationary bicycle. The pedals of the bicycle are attached to a 100 turn coil of area 0.10 m2. The coil rotates at half a revolution per second and it is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.01 T perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the coil. What is the maximum voltage generated in the coil?
solution_text: Here {tex}\nu{/tex} = 0.5 Hz; N = 100, A = 0.1 m 2 and B = 0.01 T. Employing Eq. {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \ \omega \ sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_{0}=N B A(2 \pi v){/tex} for max {tex}\epsilon {/tex} ,sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t must be equal to 1 {tex}=100 \times 0.01 \times 0.1 \times 2 \times 3.14 \times 0.5{/tex} = 0.314 V The maximum voltage is 0.314 V.
solution_html:
Here {tex}\nu{/tex} = 0.5 Hz; N = 100, A = 0.1 m2 and B = 0.01 T. Employing Eq. {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \ \omega \ sin \omega t{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon_{0}=N B A(2 \pi v){/tex} for max {tex}\epsilon {/tex}  ,sin{tex}\omega{/tex}t must be equal to 1
{tex}=100 \times 0.01 \times 0.1 \times 2 \times 3.14 \times 0.5{/tex}
= 0.314 V
The maximum voltage is 0.314 V.

subject: Physics
question_text: Predict the direction of induced current in the situations described by the following Figure.
question_html: Predict the direction of induced current in the situations described by the following Figure.
solution_text: The direction of the induced current in a closed loop is given by Lenz's law. The given pairs of figures show the direction of the induced current when the North pole of a bar magnet is moved towards and away from a closed-loop respectively. Using Lenz's rule, the direction of the induced current in the given situations can be predicted as follows: The direction of the induced current is along qrpq. The direction of the induced current is along prap. The direction of the induced current is along yzxy. The direction of the induced current is along zyxz. The direction of the induced current is along xryx. No current is induced since the field lines are lying in the plane of the closed loop.
solution_html:
The direction of the induced current in a closed loop is given by Lenz's law. The given pairs of figures show the direction of the induced current when the North pole of a bar magnet is moved towards and away from a closed-loop respectively.

Using Lenz's rule, the direction of the induced current in the given situations can be predicted as follows:

  1. The direction of the induced current is along qrpq.
  2. The direction of the induced current is along prap.
  3. The direction of the induced current is along yzxy.
  4. The direction of the induced current is along zyxz.
  5. The direction of the induced current is along xryx.
  6. No current is induced since the field lines are lying in the plane of the closed loop.

subject: Physics
question_text: Use Lenz's law to determine the direction of induced current in the situation described by figure: A wire of irregular shape turning into a circular shape. A circular loop being deformed into a narrow straight wire.
question_html:
Use Lenz's law to determine the direction of induced current in the situation described by figure:

  1. A wire of irregular shape turning into a circular shape.
  2. A circular loop being deformed into a narrow straight wire.
solution_text: When a wire of irregular shape turns into a circular loop, area of the loop tends to increase. Therefore, magnetic flux linked with the loop increases. According to Lenz's law, the direction of induced current must oppose the magnetic field, for which induced current should flow along adcba. In this case, the magnetic flux tends to decrease. Therefore, induced current must support the magnetic field for which induced current should flow along adcba.
solution_html:
1. When a wire of irregular shape turns into a circular loop, area of the loop tends to increase. Therefore, magnetic flux linked with the loop increases. According to Lenz's law, the direction of induced current must oppose the magnetic field, for which induced current should flow along adcba.
  2. In this case, the magnetic flux tends to decrease. Therefore, induced current must support the magnetic field for which induced current should flow along adcba.

subject: Physics
question_text: A long solenoid with 15 turns per cm has a small loop of area 2.0 cm 2 placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changes steadily from 2.0 A to 4.0 A in 0.1 s, what is the induced emf in the loop while the current is changing?
question_html: A long solenoid with 15 turns per cm has a small loop of area 2.0 cm2 placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changes steadily from 2.0 A to 4.0 A in 0.1 s, what is the induced emf in the loop while the current is changing?
solution_text: Given, In a solenoid, Number of turns per unit length, n = 15 turns/cm = 1500 turns/m Area of the small loop, A = 2 cm 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −4 m 2 Initial current, I 1 = 2 A Final current, I 2 = 4 A {tex}\Delta {/tex} t = 0.1 s The magnetic field associated with current I 1 . {tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{1}{/tex} The magnetic field associated with current I 2 . {tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} The change in flux associated with the change in current in solenoid, {tex}\Delta \phi=\left(B_{2}-B_{1}\right) A{/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1500 {tex}\times{/tex} (4 - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −4 = 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 weber The induced EMF while current is changing, {tex}|\mathrm{E}|=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7.6 \times 10^{-7}}{0.1}{/tex} = 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 V
solution_html:
Given, In a solenoid,
Number of turns per unit length, n = 15 turns/cm = 1500 turns/m
Area of the small loop, A = 2 cm2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−4 m2 
Initial current, I1 = 2 A Final current, I2 = 4 A
{tex}\Delta {/tex}t = 0.1 s
The magnetic field associated with current I1.
{tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{1}{/tex}
The magnetic field associated with current I2.
{tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex}
The change in flux associated with the change in current in solenoid,
{tex}\Delta \phi=\left(B_{2}-B_{1}\right) A{/tex}
= 4{tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10−7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1500 {tex}\times{/tex} (4 - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−4
= 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−7weber 
The induced EMF while current is changing,
{tex}|\mathrm{E}|=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7.6 \times 10^{-7}}{0.1}{/tex}
= 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−6 V

subject: Physics
question_text: A rectangular loop of sides 8 cm and 2 cm with a small cut is moving out of a region of uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.3 tesla directed normal to the loop. What is the voltage developed across the cut if velocity of loop is {tex}1 \; cms^{-1}{/tex} in a direction normal to the (i) longer side (ii) shorter side of the loop? For how long does the induced voltage last in each case?
question_html: A rectangular loop of sides 8 cm and 2 cm with a small cut is moving out of a region of uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.3 tesla directed normal to the loop. What is the voltage developed across the cut if velocity of loop is {tex}1 \; cms^{-1}{/tex} in a direction normal to the (i) longer side (ii) shorter side of the loop? For how long does the induced voltage last in each case?
solution_text: Given, Length of loop, l = 8 cm {tex}=8\times 10 ^{-2}\;m{/tex} Breadth of loop, b = 2 cm {tex}=2\times 10 ^{-2}\;m{/tex} Strength of magnetic field, B = 0.3 T Velocity of loop, v = 1 cm / sec {tex}=10 ^{-2}\;m/sec{/tex} Let the field be perpendicular to the plane of the paper directed inwards. The magnitude of induced emf, {tex}\varepsilon= Blv{/tex} {tex}=0.3\times 8 \times 10^{-2}\times 10 ^{-2}{/tex} {tex}=2.4\times 10^{-4} \;V{/tex} Time for which induced emf will last is equal to the time taken by the coil to move outside the field is {tex}I = \frac{{distan ce\;travelled}}{{velocity}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}{/tex} = 2 sec The conductor is moving outside the field normal to the shorter side. {tex}b=2\times 10 ^{-2} \; m{/tex} The magnitude of induced emf is {tex}\varepsilon= Bbv{/tex} {tex}=0.3\times 2 \times 10^{-2}\times 10 ^{-2}{/tex} {tex}=0.6\times 10^{-4} \; V{/tex} Time, {tex}t = \frac{{distan ce\;travelled}}{{velocity}} = \frac{{8 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}=8 \; sec{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,
Length of loop, l = 8 cm {tex}=8\times 10 ^{-2}\;m{/tex}
Breadth of loop, b = 2 cm {tex}=2\times 10 ^{-2}\;m{/tex}
Strength of magnetic field, B = 0.3 T
Velocity of loop, v = 1 cm / sec {tex}=10 ^{-2}\;m/sec{/tex}
Let the field be perpendicular to the plane of the paper directed inwards.

  1. The magnitude of induced emf,
    {tex}\varepsilon= Blv{/tex}
    {tex}=0.3\times 8 \times 10^{-2}\times 10 ^{-2}{/tex}
    {tex}=2.4\times 10^{-4} \;V{/tex}
    Time for which induced emf will last is equal to the time taken by the coil to move outside the field is
    {tex}I = \frac{{distan ce\;travelled}}{{velocity}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}{/tex} = 2 sec
  2. The conductor is moving outside the field normal to the shorter side.
    {tex}b=2\times 10 ^{-2} \; m{/tex}
    The magnitude of induced emf is
    {tex}\varepsilon= Bbv{/tex}
    {tex}=0.3\times 2 \times 10^{-2}\times 10 ^{-2}{/tex}
    {tex}=0.6\times 10^{-4} \; V{/tex}
    Time, {tex}t = \frac{{distan ce\;travelled}}{{velocity}} = \frac{{8 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}=8 \; sec{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A 1.0 m long conducting rod rotates with an angular frequency of 400 rad s -1 about an axis normal to the rod passing through its one end. The other end of the rod is in contact with a circular metallic ring. A constant magnetic field of 0.5 T parallel to the axis exists everywhere. Calculate the emf developed between the centre and the ring.
question_html: A 1.0 m long conducting rod rotates with an angular frequency of 400 rad s-1 about an axis normal to the rod passing through its one end. The other end of the rod is in contact with a circular metallic ring. A constant magnetic field of 0.5 T parallel to the axis exists everywhere. Calculate the emf developed between the centre and the ring.
solution_text: Here, l = 1 m, {tex}\omega = 400\; rad{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} , B = 0.5 T, e = ? Note that linear velocity of one end of rod is zero and linear velocity of other end is {tex}\left( {l\omega } \right){/tex} . Average linear velocity {tex}v = \frac{{0 + l\omega }}{2} = \frac{{l\omega }}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore e = Blv = Bl\frac{{\left( {l\omega } \right)}}{2} = \frac{{B{l^2}\omega }}{2} = \frac{{0.5 \times {1^2} \times 400}}{2}=100{/tex} V
solution_html:
Here, l = 1 m, {tex}\omega = 400\; rad{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}, B = 0.5 T, e = ?
Note that linear velocity of one end of rod is zero and linear velocity of other end is {tex}\left( {l\omega } \right){/tex}. Average linear velocity
{tex}v = \frac{{0 + l\omega }}{2} = \frac{{l\omega }}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore e = Blv = Bl\frac{{\left( {l\omega } \right)}}{2} = \frac{{B{l^2}\omega }}{2} = \frac{{0.5 \times {1^2} \times 400}}{2}=100{/tex} V

subject: Physics
question_text: A horizontal straight wire 10 m long extending from east to west is falling with a speed of 5.0 ms -1 , at right angles to the horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field, 0.30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 Wb m -2 . What is the instantaneous value of the emf induced in the wire? What is the direction of the emf? Which end of the wire is at the higher electrical potential?
question_html:
A horizontal straight wire 10 m long extending from east to west is falling with a speed of 5.0 ms-1, at right angles to the horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field, 0.30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 Wb m-2.

  1. What is the instantaneous value of the emf induced in the wire?
  2. What is the direction of the emf?
  3. Which end of the wire is at the higher electrical potential?
solution_text: Length of the wire, l = 10 m Falling speed of the wire, v = 5.0 m/s Magnetic field strength, B = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 wb m -2 the instantaneous value of Emf induced in the wire, e = Blv {tex}=0.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 V Using Fleming's right-hand rule, it can be inferred that the direction of the induced emf is from West to East. The eastern end of the wire is at a higher potential.
solution_html:
Length of the wire, l = 10 m
Falling speed of the wire, v = 5.0 m/s
Magnetic field strength, B = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 wb m-2

  1. the instantaneous value of Emf induced in the wire,
    e = Blv
    {tex}=0.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10{/tex}
    = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 V
  2. Using Fleming's right-hand rule, it can be inferred that the direction of the induced emf is from West to East.
  3. The eastern end of the wire is at a higher potential.

subject: Physics
question_text: Current in a circuit falls from 5.0 A to 0.0 A in 0.1 s. If an average emf of 200 V induced, give an estimate of the self inductance of the circuit?
question_html: Current in a circuit falls from 5.0 A to 0.0 A in 0.1 s. If an average emf of 200 V induced, give an estimate of the self inductance of the circuit?
solution_text: Here, {tex}\frac{dI}{dt}=\frac{(I_2-I_1)}{t}=\frac{0.0-5.0}{0.1}=-50\;As^{-1}{/tex} e = 200 V, L = ? As {tex}\left| e \right| = L\left| {\frac{{dI}}{{dt}}} \right|\;\;\therefore \;\;L = \frac{{\left| e \right|}}{{\left| {\frac{{dI}}{{dt}}} \right|}} = \frac{{200}}{{50}}{/tex} = 4 H
solution_html:
Here,{tex}\frac{dI}{dt}=\frac{(I_2-I_1)}{t}=\frac{0.0-5.0}{0.1}=-50\;As^{-1}{/tex}
e = 200 V, L = ?
As {tex}\left| e \right| = L\left| {\frac{{dI}}{{dt}}} \right|\;\;\therefore \;\;L = \frac{{\left| e \right|}}{{\left| {\frac{{dI}}{{dt}}} \right|}} = \frac{{200}}{{50}}{/tex} = 4 H

subject: Physics
question_text: A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of 1.5 H. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to 20 A in 0.5 s, what is the change of flux linkage with the other coil?
question_html: A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of 1.5 H. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to 20 A in 0.5 s, what is the change of flux linkage with the other coil?
solution_text: Mutual inductance of a pair of could, {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 H Initial current I 1 = 0A Final current I 2 = 20A Change in current in the circuit is given by, dI = I 2 - I 1 = 20 - 0 = 20 A Time taken for the change, t = 0.5 S Induced emf, {tex}e=\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ..... (i) Where {tex}d \phi{/tex} is the change in the flux linkage with the coil. Emf is related with mutual inductance as: {tex}e=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} ..... (ii) Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} {tex}d \phi=1.5 \times(20){/tex} = 30 Wb Hence, the change in the flux linkage is 30 Wb.
solution_html:
Mutual inductance of a pair of could, {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 H
Initial current I1 = 0A
Final current I2 = 20A
Change in current in the circuit is given by, dI = I2 - I1 = 20 - 0 = 20 A
Time taken for the change, t = 0.5 S
Induced emf, {tex}e=\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ..... (i)
Where {tex}d \phi{/tex} is the change in the flux linkage with the coil.
Emf is related with mutual inductance as:
{tex}e=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} ..... (ii)
Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}d \phi=1.5 \times(20){/tex}
= 30 Wb
Hence, the change in the flux linkage is 30 Wb.
